Power-On to Code Execution  
Microcontroller with  
Bare-Metal Code  
OS-Based Systems  
Boot ROM  
Reset  
Reset Handler  
Startup Code  
Calling main()  
Bootloader  
Kernel  
User Application  
▪ On a microcontroller, there is no operating system.  
Boot ROM = manufacturer-provided startup  
code stored in on-chip read-only memory.  
▪ The CPU does exactly one thing: It jumps directly into your code.

variables live in Flash; copy them to RAM  
destination.

values.

(often remapped)  
The Super-Loop Model  
Consider sample example program  
If each function takes 10 ms, what is the loop frequency?  
int main(void)  
{
init_uart();  
init_sensor();  
init_led();  
Key Observations  
How many  
programs are  
running?  
Who decides  
What decides  
timing?  
while (1)  
{
what runs next?  
read_sensor();  
process_data();  
send_uart();  
toggle_led();  
delay_ms(100);  
}
Delays and  
timers  
One  
You  
}
This model works well for tiny systems but collapses as features grow.  
The Blocking Problem  
• Consider sample example program  
int main(void)  
{
“When UART blocks for 300 ms, what happens to sensor sampling  
cadence?”  
init_uart();  
init_sensor();  
init_led();  
“If you need sensor sampling every 50 ms, can this meet the deadline?”  
while (1)  
{
•
•
•
Delayed sampling violates timing guarantees—late samples are wrong for  
control systems.  
read_sensor();  
delay_ms(50)  
send_uart();  
delay_ms(300)  
toggle_led();  
delay_ms(10);  
}
You cannot guarantee periodic sampling with blocking calls in a serialized  
loop.  
Reordering does not fix the fundamental serialization.  
}
Blocking in one feature propagates to all features in a super-loop.  
Limitations of Super-Loop Firmware  
Scalability  
What happens when you add:  
• Ethernet?  
Maintainability  
“You want to add a logging feature. You add calls in the  
loop and a few global flags.  
• USB?  
Two months later, a teammate removes an obsolete  
feature. Now logging breaks.”  
• Logging?  
• Display?  
•
•
One change affects everything, Hard to add/remove features  
Tight coupling in shared globals and timing assumptions  
cause hidden dependencies.  
→ The loop grows… and grows… and breaks.  
Adding functions increases latency unpredictably—each  
function’s run-time adds up in the loop.  
Feature Isolation  
Reliability  
Sensor code and networking code share buffers and  
timing. Networking stalls; sensor data stops being  
sampled on time.  
A single bug (e.g., NULL pointer in display code) crashes  
the entire controller. Is there any containment?  
No recovery mechanism  
•
•
•
In bare-metal systems, every feature trusts every other  
feature.  
Double buffers help data flow but do not isolate faults or  
guarantee scheduling.  
Priority in bare metal is manual and fragile; if high-priority  
work starves others, they miss deadlines.  
•
Watchdogs only reset—they do not contain faults or preserve  
state.  
•
•
Defensive coding helps but cannot isolate failures.  
Bare-metal has no memory protection—a write out of  
bounds corrupts unrelated features.  
Interrupt Driven Design  
Main  
program  
running  
Interrupt  
Service  
Routine  
Interrupt Occurs  
Main program  
continues  
• Interrupts do not create parallelism.  
• Interrupts only pause the main loop — they do not replace it.  
• Interrupt based design will not solve the super loop concerns.  
Interrupts help responsiveness, not concurrency.  
Interrupts help responsiveness but do not provide isolation or scheduling guarantees.  
Scenario - Situation  
(1/4)  
Develop a solution for designing Smart Industrial  
Controller  
Constraints:  
• Single super-loop only, no OS (bare-metal mindset).  
• You may use interrupts for sensor sampling.  
• No multitasking, no threads/processes.  
• Assume blocking I/O for networking and flash.  
Features:  
• Sensor sampling: Read temperature every 50 ms;  
processing must finish within 10 ms of each sample.  
• Display update: Refresh UI at 10 Hz (every 100 ms).  
• Alarm monitoring: Check thresholds continuously;  
Tasks:  
alarm must be raised within 20 ms.  
• Decide order and delays.  
• Networking: Send telemetry packets every 500 ms;  
network stack may block up to 200 ms per send.  
• If using an ISR for sensor sampling, specify ISR duration  
and main-loop interactions.  
• Logging: Write to flash every 1 minute; flash erase can  
block for 300 ms.  
• Identify worst-case latencies for each feature.  
• Predict failures under load (e.g., slow network send + flash  
logging overlap).  
Scenario - Situation  
(2/4)  
Requirements:  
Feature  
Sensor sampling  
Display  
Period  
Deadline  
Worst-Case Blocking  
50 ms  
10 ms processing  
soft deadline  
20 ms  
ISR + main loop delay  
100 ms  
3–5 ms typical  
Alarm  
continuous  
500 ms  
Depends on network/logging blocking  
200 ms blocking  
Network  
no hard deadline  
none  
Flash logging  
1 minute  
300 ms blocking  
Timeline  
Sensor sampling |-----50 ms -----|-----50 ms -----|-----50 ms -----|-----50 ms -----|  
Display  
|--------------100 ms --------------|--------------100 ms --------------|  
Network  
|--------------------------------------500 ms ---------------------------------|  
|------------------------------------------------------1 min----------------------------------------------|  
Flash logging  
Scenario – Typical Solution  
(3/4)  
while(1) {  
“We’ll ensure sensor is first so it’s timely.”  
“We’ll put logging at the end so it doesn’t interfere.”  
“We’ll run alarm checks very frequently.”  
“Which deadlines were violated and why?”  
read_sensor(); // 10 ms  
process_data(); // 5 ms  
display_update(); // 3 ms  
alarm_check(); // 2 ms  
network_send(); // 200 ms (blocking worst case)  
log_to_flash_if_due(); // 300 ms (blocking worst case)  
delay_ms(10); // try to pace loop  
}
“Could you guarantee alarm response within 20 ms  
during logging?”  
“Could you guarantee sensor processing within 10 ms  
when network blocks?”  
•
•
•
deadlines were violated due to blocking and serialized work.  
Alarm response cannot be guaranteed in presence of 200–300 ms blocking operations.  
Sensor processing cannot be guaranteed because even if sampling is in ISR, post-processing and  
actuation depended on main loop.  
Scenario - The Solution  
(4/4)  
Scheduling  
Isolation  
• avoids blocking delays  
• memory protection between  
components  
Abstractions  
• drivers, file systems, sockets  
Bare-metal Problem → OS Solution  
Bare-metal Problem  
One big loop → serialization  
Blocking network/logging  
No isolation  
OS Solution  
Preemptive scheduling  
Threads + asynchronous I/O  
Memory protection  
Fault in one function kills system  
Feature interactions  
Process isolation / watchdog restart  
Drivers + queues + buffers  
OS tick / scheduler  
Manual timing  
• An OS doesn’t eliminate blocking — it isolates it so the rest of the system continues running.  
• As features scale, concurrency, isolation, and scheduling become mandatory — a super-loop can no  
longer meet system requirements.  
Operating System Benifits  
A complex embedded product requires an Operating System to provide:  
Scheduling  
Isolation  
Who runs when  
preemption, priorities  
process boundaries, memory  
protection  
One bug ≠ total failure  
Uniform hardware access  
non-blocking, async,  
buffered  
Abstractions  
Reliability  
Predictable timing under  
load and/or real-time  
extensions  
Controlled failures  
Summary  
Super-loop =  
simple but  
fragile  
Interrupts ≠  
concurrency  
Real systems  
need a  
Blocking  
destroys  
timing  
scheduler +  
isolation
